questions.questionText: A 40-year-old man presents with sudden severe abdominal pain. Erect CXR shows free gas under both hemidiaphragms (pneumoperitoneum). The most common cause is:
questions.optionB: Perforated peptic ulcer (duodenal > gastric — most common cause of pneumoperitoneum)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Pneumoperitoneum = hollow viscus perforation, peptic ulcer is most common cause. Why correct: Anterior duodenal ulcer perforation is commonest cause. Free gas seen as radiolucent crescent under diaphragm on erect CXR. Why A wrong: Appendicular perforation may cause localized abscess but less often free pneumoperitoneum. Why C wrong: Typhoid perforation affects terminal ileum — more common in developing countries but overall less common than peptic ulcer. Why D wrong: Sigmoid volvulus perforation is uncommon. Clinical relevance: Rigler sign (supine): double-wall sign — gas on both sides of bowel wall. Only 1mL of gas is detectable. Left lateral decubitus if patient can't sit up. Exam trap: Post-laparoscopy/laparotomy, pneumoperitoneum is NORMAL for up to 7 days — don't misdiagnose as perforation. Memory aid: 'Pneumoperitoneum = Peptic Perforation predominantly.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: Supine abdominal X-ray of a neonate shows a 'double-bubble' sign. This is characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Duodenal atresia (complete obstruction — distended stomach + proximal duodenum with no distal gas, associated with Down syndrome)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Double-bubble sign = dilated stomach + proximal duodenum, no distal gas = duodenal atresia. Why correct: Complete duodenal obstruction → air in stomach (first bubble) + proximal duodenum (second bubble). No gas beyond. 30% associated with Down syndrome. Why A wrong: Pyloric stenosis — presents at 3-6 weeks (not neonate), single gastric bubble with 'caterpillar sign,' USG shows pyloric muscle thickness >3mm. Why C wrong: Jejunal atresia — triple-bubble sign or multiple dilated loops. Why D wrong: Hirschsprung — distal obstruction, dilated proximal bowel, delayed meconium passage. Clinical relevance: Bilious vomiting on day 1 of life. Other causes of duodenal obstruction: annular pancreas, duodenal web, Ladd's bands (malrotation). Exam trap: Double-bubble WITH distal gas = incomplete obstruction (duodenal stenosis/malrotation), not atresia. Memory aid: 'Double-Bubble = Duodenal + Down syndrome.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: A 70-year-old male presents with abdominal distension and absolute constipation. X-ray abdomen shows a massively dilated loop of colon arising from the pelvis with a 'coffee-bean' sign pointing toward the right upper quadrant. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Coffee-bean sign arising from pelvis + pointing RUQ = sigmoid volvulus. Why correct: Sigmoid volvulus — axial twist of sigmoid colon on its mesentery → massively dilated ahaustral loop with convergent walls at twist point ('coffee-bean'). Arises from pelvis, extends toward RUQ. Why A wrong: Cecal volvulus — coffee-bean but arising from RIF and pointing toward LUQ (opposite direction). Why C wrong: Carcinoma — 'apple-core' lesion on barium enema, proximal dilatation with transition point. Why D wrong: Toxic megacolon — diffuse colonic dilatation without volvulus, associated with IBD. Clinical relevance: Treatment: sigmoidoscopic decompression (rectal tube) → elective sigmoid colectomy. If gangrenous → emergency surgery. Exam trap: Sigmoid volvulus → pelvic origin → RUQ apex. Cecal volvulus → RIF origin → LUQ apex. Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: Multiple air-fluid levels at different heights on erect abdominal X-ray with distended small bowel loops (>3cm) and collapsed large bowel suggest:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Multiple air-fluid levels at DIFFERENT heights + dilated small bowel + collapsed large bowel = mechanical SBO. Why correct: Mechanical obstruction → proximal dilatation with multiple air-fluid levels at varying heights (step-ladder pattern). Small bowel: central, valvulae conniventes (cross entire lumen). Why A wrong: Paralytic ileus — BOTH small AND large bowel dilated, air-fluid levels at SAME height, no transition point. Why C wrong: LBO — dilated large bowel (peripheral, haustral folds not crossing full lumen), small bowel may dilate if ileocecal valve incompetent. Why D wrong: Pneumoperitoneum — free gas, not air-fluid levels. Clinical relevance: Transition point identifies obstruction site. Most common cause of SBO: adhesions (post-surgery). LBO: carcinoma. Exam trap: Different levels = mechanical; Same levels = paralytic. This is a HIGH-YIELD differentiator. Memory aid: 'Mechanical = Multiple levels at Mixed heights; Ileus = Identical heights.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: USG of the right upper quadrant shows a round, echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing in the gallbladder. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Gallstone (cholelithiasis — echogenic with clean posterior acoustic shadow, moves with position change)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Echogenic focus + posterior acoustic shadow + gravity-dependent movement = gallstone. Why correct: Gallstones are highly echogenic (reflect ultrasound) → create clean posterior acoustic shadow. They move with position change (gravity-dependent). USG sensitivity >95% for gallstones. Why A wrong: Polyps are echogenic but WITHOUT shadow (too small), and do NOT move with position change (attached to wall). Why C wrong: Sludge — low-level echoes layering dependently, no discrete shadow. Why D wrong: Carcinoma — irregular thickened wall or mass, may have stones associated. Clinical relevance: WES triad (Wall-Echo-Shadow) — contracted gallbladder packed with stones. Murphy sign on USG: focal tenderness under transducer over gallbladder = cholecystitis. Exam trap: Gallstone WITH wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid = acute cholecystitis. Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: A child with acute RIF pain. USG shows a non-compressible, blind-ending tubular structure in the RIF with diameter >6mm and surrounding echogenic fat. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Non-compressible appendix >6mm on USG = acute appendicitis. Why correct: USG criteria for appendicitis: diameter >6mm, non-compressible, blind-ending tubular structure, periappendiceal fat stranding/echogenic fat, ± appendicolith. Why A wrong: Intussusception — target/doughnut sign (transverse), pseudokidney sign (longitudinal). Why C wrong: Mesenteric lymphadenitis — multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes, normal appendix. Why D wrong: Meckel's — usually an incidental finding, diagnosed by Tc-99m pertechnetate scan (ectopic gastric mucosa). Clinical relevance: CT is more sensitive in adults; USG preferred in children/pregnant women (no radiation). Alvarado score for clinical assessment. Exam trap: Normal appendix diameter <6mm. 6-8mm is borderline. >8mm strongly suggests appendicitis. Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: Triple-phase contrast CT of the liver shows an arterially enhancing lesion with centripetal fill-in on portal venous and delayed phases. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Hemangioma (peripheral nodular arterial enhancement with progressive centripetal fill-in — 'light-bulb' bright on T2 MRI)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Peripheral arterial enhancement + progressive centripetal fill-in = hemangioma. Why correct: Hemangioma — most common benign liver tumor. Enhancement pattern: peripheral discontinuous nodular enhancement in arterial phase → progressive centripetal (toward center) fill-in on venous/delayed phases. MRI: 'light-bulb' bright on T2 (fluid content). Why A wrong: HCC — arterial enhancement + WASHOUT on portal venous (not fill-in). Why C wrong: FNH — arterial enhancement with central scar (enhances on delayed), spoke-wheel vascularity. Why D wrong: Hepatic adenoma — early homogeneous enhancement, may hemorrhage. Clinical relevance: Hemangiomas are usually incidental, no treatment needed unless symptomatic. Exam trap: Centripetal fill-in (hemangioma) vs washout (HCC) — direction and timing of enhancement is key. Memory aid: 'Hemangioma = fills like a bathtub — from edges inward.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.

questions.questionText: CT abdomen shows 'whirl sign' with twisting of mesenteric vessels and proximal bowel dilatation. This finding is characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Midgut volvulus (small bowel volvulus around the SMA axis — twisting of mesentery creates whirl pattern)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Whirl sign on CT = mesenteric volvulus (twisting of mesentery/vessels). Why correct: Clockwise or counterclockwise twisting of mesenteric fat and vessels around a fixed point → 'whirl' pattern. Seen in midgut malrotation with volvulus, sigmoid volvulus, and cecal volvulus. Why A wrong: Intussusception — target/doughnut sign on CT. Why C wrong: Adhesive SBO — transition point but no mesenteric whirl. Why D wrong: Mesenteric ischemia — bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis, portal venous gas but no whirl. Clinical relevance: In neonates, upper GI series shows 'corkscrew/coiled-spring' duodenum for malrotation. Ladd's procedure for malrotation. Exam trap: Whirl sign can also be seen in internal hernia and small bowel volvulus — context matters. Memory aid: 'Whirl = Wound-up mesentery = Volvulus.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Abdomen.
questions.conceptTag: Whirl sign volvulus mesenteric twisting CT
